guide.title: Guide
guide.search_hint: Press Ctrl+F (or ⌘+F on Mac) to search this page.
guide.q.create_listing: How do I create an listing?
guide.a.create_listing: From the Listings page, fill in the form at the bottom. Give your listing a name, set the capacity, and choose which contact details to collect (any combination of email, phone, postal address, and special instructions — or none at all for name-only registration). You can leave the price blank for free listings. Once created, share the booking link with your attendees.
guide.q.setup_payments: How do I set up payments?
guide.a.setup_payments: Go to Settings and choose Stripe or Square as your payment provider. Paste in your API key and save. For Stripe, the webhook is configured automatically. For Square, you'll need to copy the webhook URL shown and add it in your Square Developer Dashboard.
guide.a.what_is_dashboard: The Listings page is your dashboard. It lists all your listings with attendee counts, booking links, and quick actions. At the top, the Active Listing Statistics section shows totals across all active listings: total income, number of ticket rows, and total attendee quantity. Click the heading to expand or collapse it.
guide.q.test_after_changing_settings: Should I test after changing settings?
guide.a.test_after_changing_settings: Yes. After changing any setting — especially payment configuration, listing capacity, or booking fields — you should test the full booking process to make sure everything works as expected. Create a test listing (or use test payment credentials) and complete a booking from start to finish.
guide.q.report_bug: How do I report a bug?
guide.a.report_bug: This project is early in development (started January 2026) and bugs are expected. If you find something that doesn't work correctly, please email hello@chobble.com with a description of what happened and what you expected. The more detail you can provide, the faster it can be fixed.
guide.q.standard_vs_daily_listings: What’s the difference between standard and daily listings?
guide.a.standard_vs_daily_listings: A standard listing is a one-off — attendees book a place and the capacity applies to the whole listing. A daily listing lets attendees pick a specific date when booking. The capacity limit applies separately to each date, so you can run the same listing every day with a fresh allocation.
guide.q.combine_multiple_listings: How do I combine multiple listings into one booking?
guide.a.combine_multiple_listings:
Join listing slugs with a + in the URL, e.g. /ticket/listing-one+listing-two. Attendees see a single form, fill in their details once, and book all selected listings in one go. If any are paid, they complete one checkout for the total.

To generate the link, open the Multi-booking link section on the Listings page and tick the listings you want to combine. The link updates as you select, and listings appear in the order you tick them.
guide.a.what_are_groups: Groups let you bundle related listings under a single URL. Create a group from the Groups page, then assign listings to it using the group dropdown on the listing form. Share /ticket/your-group-slug and attendees see all active listings in the group on one page. If you add terms and conditions to a group, they replace the global T&Cs for that page.
guide.q.listing_date_and_location: What are the listing date and location fields for?
guide.a.listing_date_and_location: These are optional fields you can fill in when creating or editing an listing. The date is when the listing takes place (in your configured timezone) and the location is where it's held. Both are displayed on the attendee's ticket page so they know when and where to go. For daily listings, attendees already pick a date when booking, so the listing date field is more useful for standard (one-off) listings.
guide.q.max_tickets_per_purchase: What does ‘max tickets per purchase’ do?
guide.a.max_tickets_per_purchase: It controls how many tickets one person can book in a single transaction. For example, setting it to 4 lets someone book up to 4 places at once. The quantity dropdown on the booking form won't exceed this number or the remaining capacity, whichever is lower.
guide.a.allow_pay_more: When enabled, attendees can choose their own price instead of paying a fixed amount. The ticket price becomes a minimum. You can set a maximum price using the "Maximum Price" field — it must be at least {minPriceFormatted} more than the ticket price. If the ticket price is zero, it becomes a pay-what-you-want listing where attendees can optionally enter any amount up to the configured maximum.
guide.a.registration_deadlines: Set a "closes at" date and time on your listing. After that moment, the booking form shows a "registration closed" message and no further bookings are accepted. If someone loaded the form before the deadline but submits after, their booking is also rejected.
guide.q.embed_booking_form: How do I embed the booking form on my website?
guide.a.embed_booking_form:
You only need one of the two embed codes shown on your listing page — not both:

  • Embed Script (recommended) — paste a single <script> tag and it creates the iframe for you with automatic resizing.
  • Embed Iframe — use this if your hosting platform blocks third-party scripts or if you want full control over the iframe styling yourself.

In Settings, add your website's domain to the embed hosts list so the browser allows the iframe to load.
guide.q.manually_add_attendee: How do I manually add an attendee?
guide.a.manually_add_attendee: Open the listing page and scroll down to Add Attendee . Fill in the name and contact details, set the quantity, and submit. The attendee is added directly without needing to go through the booking form or payment flow. Useful for walk-ins, comps, or manual corrections.
guide.q.custom_redirect_after_booking: How do I set a custom redirect after booking?
guide.a.custom_redirect_after_booking: When creating or editing an listing, enter a URL in the "thank you URL" field. After a successful booking or payment, attendees are redirected to that address instead of seeing the default confirmation page. The URL must use HTTPS.
guide.q.add_listing_image: How do I add an image to an listing?
guide.a.add_listing_image: When creating or editing an listing, use the image upload field to attach a picture. The image is displayed on the booking page and in the public listings listing. Supported formats include JPEG and PNG.
guide.q.add_file_attachment: How do I add a file attachment to an listing?
guide.a.add_file_attachment:
When creating or editing an listing, use the attachment upload field to attach a file. This can be any type of file — PDFs, Word documents, spreadsheets, images, audio, video, zip archives, you name it. The maximum file size is 25 MB.

Attendees see a download link on their ticket page. The link is time-limited and unique to each attendee, so it can't be shared or reused by others. Each time an attendee visits their ticket page, they get a fresh link that works for a short window.

To remove an attachment, open the listing and click Delete next to the current file name.
guide.q.listing_qr_code: Where can I find the listing QR code?
guide.a.listing_qr_code: On the admin listing page, click the QR code link next to the public URL. This opens an SVG image of the QR code that links to your listing's registration page. You can save or print it for posters, flyers, or other materials.
guide.q.duplicate_listing: How do I duplicate an listing?
guide.a.duplicate_listing: Open the listing and click Duplicate. This creates a new listing with the same settings (name, capacity, price, fields, etc.) so you can adjust what you need without starting from scratch.
guide.q.deactivate_listing: How do I deactivate an listing?
guide.a.deactivate_listing: Open the listing and click Deactivate. Deactivated listings no longer accept bookings and are hidden from the public listings listing, but all existing attendee data is kept. Click Reactivate to make the listing bookable again.
guide.a.non_transferable_tickets: When you enable Non-Transferable on an listing, attendees see a notice on their ticket saying "Non-transferable — ID required at entry". At check-in, the QR scanner prompts door staff to verify the attendee's ID matches the name on the ticket before completing check-in. This helps prevent ticket touting.
guide.q.edit_attendee: How do I edit an attendee?
guide.a.edit_attendee: Open the listing's attendee list, find the attendee, and click Edit. You can update their name, email, phone, address, special instructions, and quantity. You can also reassign them to a different listing using the listing dropdown. Quantity changes are validated against the listing's capacity and maximum tickets per purchase.
guide.q.add_terms_and_conditions: How do I add terms and conditions?
guide.a.add_terms_and_conditions: In Settings, enter your terms in the "Terms and Conditions" box. When set, attendees must tick an agreement checkbox before they can reserve tickets. Clear the box to remove the requirement.
guide.a.what_are_custom_booking_question: Custom booking questions let you ask attendees a multiple-choice question during the booking process. Each question has a set of answers you define, and the attendee must select one before they can complete their booking.
guide.q.create_question: How do I create a question?
guide.a.create_question: Go to Questions in the admin menu, type your question text, and click Add Question. Then open the question and add your answer options. You can reorder answers using the move-up and move-down buttons.
guide.q.add_question_to_listing: How do I add a question to an listing?
guide.a.add_question_to_listing: Open the listing in the admin area and click Questions. Tick the questions you want to appear on that listing's booking form and save. The same question can be shared across multiple listings — create it once and assign it wherever you need it.
guide.q.share_questions_between_listings: Can I share questions between listings?
guide.a.share_questions_between_listings: Yes. Questions are created independently and then assigned to listings, so a single question can appear on as many listings as you like. Updating the question text or answers updates it everywhere it's used.
guide.q.where_to_see_answers: Where do I see the answers?
guide.a.where_to_see_answers: Answers appear in the attendee table on listing and group pages, so you can see at a glance what each attendee chose. They're also shown on the individual attendee detail page, included in the CSV export, and sent in webhook payloads.
guide.q.facebook_403_error: Why do I get a 403 error when sharing my link on Facebook?
guide.a.facebook_403_error:
When you first share a link from a new domain on Facebook, Facebook may return a 403 error. This is because Facebook doesn't recognise the domain yet — the error comes from Facebook itself and doesn't even reach your site.

To fix this, paste your public link into the Facebook Sharing Debugger and click Scrape Again if the result looks wrong. Once Facebook has successfully scraped the URL, future shares should work normally.
guide.a.what_is_public_site: When enabled in Settings, your domain shows a public website with navigation for Home, Listings, Terms & Conditions, and Contact pages. The Listings page lists all active listings with booking links. Visitors can browse listing details and book directly. If the public site is disabled, visitors can still book via direct ticket links.
guide.q.hide_listing_from_public_list: Can I hide an listing from the public list?
guide.a.hide_listing_from_public_list: Yes. When editing an listing, tick the Hidden Listing checkbox. Hidden listings won't appear on the public Listings page and their ticket pages will be marked as noindex, nofollow for search engines. The listing is still fully bookable via its direct link or when embedded in an iframe.
guide.q.edit_homepage_and_contact: How do I edit the homepage and contact page?
guide.a.edit_homepage_and_contact: Enable the public site in Settings, then open the Site section from the admin navigation. The homepage editor lets you set a website title (shown as the heading on all public pages) and homepage text. The contact page editor lets you set contact information. Line breaks are preserved in both.
guide.a.fields_support_formatting: Listing descriptions, terms and conditions, homepage text, and contact page text all support Markdown formatting.
guide.a.what_formatting_can_i_use: Bold with **bold**, italic with *italic*, links with [text](https://...), and lists with - at the start of a line. See the Markdown cheat sheet for more options.
guide.a.supported_payment_providers: Stripe and Square. Choose one in Settings and enter your API credentials. You can switch between them at any time.
guide.q.recommended_payment_provider: Which payment provider do you recommend?
guide.a.recommended_payment_provider: Stripe. The setup is a fair bit easier — you just paste in your secret key and the webhook is created automatically. With Square you need to create a developer application, find your location ID, and manually configure the webhook yourself. Both work well once set up, but Stripe gets you going faster.
guide.q.paid_ticket_booking_flow: What happens when someone books a paid ticket?
guide.a.paid_ticket_booking_flow: They fill in the booking form, then are redirected to your payment provider's checkout page. Their place is held for 5 minutes while they pay. Once payment is confirmed, the booking is finalised and they receive their ticket.
guide.a.reservation_window: When someone starts paying for a ticket, their place is reserved for 5 minutes. If they don't complete payment within that time, the reservation is released and the place becomes available for others. This prevents places being held indefinitely by abandoned checkouts.
guide.q.listing_sells_out_while_paying: What if the listing sells out while someone is paying?
guide.a.listing_sells_out_while_paying: If someone completes payment but the listing has since sold out, they are automatically refunded. They'll see a message explaining the listing is full and that their payment has been returned.
guide.a.how_refunds_work: See the Refunds section below for full details on automatic refunds, admin-issued refunds, and bulk refunds.
guide.a.what_is_booking_fee:
The booking fee is an optional percentage-based charge added to ticket prices at checkout. For example, if you set a 2% booking fee on a {ticketPriceFormatted} ticket, the attendee pays {totalWithFeeFormatted} in total.

Configure it in Settings under Booking Fee (only visible when a payment provider is set up). Enter a percentage between 0 and 10. Set it to 0 or leave it blank to disable. The fee is calculated on the subtotal and added automatically during checkout.
guide.q.find_stripe_secret_key: How do I find my Stripe secret key?
guide.a.find_stripe_secret_key:
1. Log in to your Stripe Dashboard
  2. Click Developers in the top navigation bar
  3. Select API keys from the sidebar
  4. Under Standard keys, find the Secret key row and click Reveal
  5. Copy the key — it starts with sk_live_ for live mode or sk_test_ for test mode
  6. Paste it into the Stripe Secret Key field on the Settings page

Tip: Use a sk_test_ key first to verify everything works before switching to your live key. Test mode lets you make bookings without real charges.
guide.q.stripe_webhook_setup: Do I need to set up a Stripe webhook myself?
guide.a.stripe_webhook_setup: No. When you save your Stripe secret key in Settings, the system automatically creates a webhook endpoint in your Stripe account and stores the signing secret. You can verify it's working by clicking the Test Connection button that appears after saving your key.
guide.q.create_square_application: How do I create a Square application?
guide.a.create_square_application:
Before you can accept payments with Square, you need to create an application in the Square Developer Dashboard. This gives you the API credentials the system needs.

  1. Go to the Square Developer Dashboard and sign in with your Square account
  2. Click the + button to create a new application
  3. Give it a name (e.g. your organisation or listing name) and click Save

Once created, you can find your access token and location ID from the application's pages (see below).
guide.q.find_square_access_token: How do I find my Square access token?
guide.a.find_square_access_token:
1. Log in to the Square Developer Dashboard
  2. Select your application
  3. Open the Credentials page
  4. Copy the Access token for the environment you want (Sandbox for testing, Production for real payments)
  5. Paste it into the Square Access Token field on the Settings page
guide.q.find_square_location_id: How do I find my Square location ID?
guide.a.find_square_location_id:
1. Log in to the Square Developer Dashboard and select your application
  2. Open the Locations page in the sidebar
  3. Copy the Location ID for the location you want to accept payments at

You can also find your location ID in the main Square Dashboard under Account & Settings → Business information → Locations.
guide.q.setup_square_webhook: How do I set up the Square webhook?
guide.a.setup_square_webhook:
Unlike Stripe, the Square webhook must be configured manually. After saving your Square access token and location ID:

  1. Go to the Square Developer Dashboard and select your application
  2. Navigate to Webhooks in the sidebar
  3. Click Add Subscription
  4. Set the Notification URL to the webhook URL shown on your Settings page
  5. Subscribe to the payment.updated listing
  6. Save the subscription, then copy the Signature Key that Square provides
  7. Paste the signature key into the webhook field on your Settings page

The webhook is what tells the system when a payment has been completed, so bookings won't be confirmed until this is set up.
guide.q.stripe_test_vs_live_keys: What is the difference between Stripe test and live keys?
guide.a.stripe_test_vs_live_keys:
Stripe provides two completely separate environments, each with its own set of API keys:

  • Test mode (sk_test_ / pk_test_) — a sandbox environment for development and testing. No real money is moved. You can use Stripe's test card numbers to simulate successful and failed payments. Bookings created in test mode won't appear in your live Stripe dashboard.
  • Live mode (sk_live_ / pk_live_) — the production environment. Real cards are charged and real money is transferred to your Stripe account.

The system detects which mode you are in based on the key prefix and shows it on the Settings page. Only keys with a valid prefix (sk_test_ or sk_live_) are accepted.
guide.q.test_or_live_credentials: Should I use test or live credentials?
guide.a.test_or_live_credentials:
Start with test credentials to make sure everything is working before accepting real payments. Both Stripe and Square provide separate test environments:

  • Stripe: Use a key starting with sk_test_. You can make test payments with Stripe's test card numbers . The Settings page will show a Test mode badge so you always know which environment is active.
  • Square: Use your Sandbox access token and location, and tick the Sandbox mode checkbox on the Settings page. You can make test payments with Square's sandbox test values . Untick Sandbox mode when switching to production.

When you're ready to go live, replace the test credentials with your production credentials in Settings. The system will only accept keys that start with a valid prefix (sk_test_ or sk_live_).
guide.a.automatic_refunds: Automatic refunds happen in two scenarios. First, when an listing sells out while someone is completing payment. Their place is held for 5 minutes during checkout, but if another buyer fills the last spot first, the slower payer is automatically refunded and shown a message explaining the listing is full. Second, if an admin changes the listing price while someone is mid-checkout. The system detects that the amount charged no longer matches the current price and refunds the payment automatically. In multi-listing bookings, if any single listing fails (e.g. one of the combined listings is full or its price changed), the entire payment is refunded — not just the portion for the affected listing.
guide.q.refund_individual_attendee: How do I refund an individual attendee?
guide.a.refund_individual_attendee: Open the listing's attendee list, find the attendee, and click Refund. You'll see a confirmation page showing their name, email, quantity, and the amount paid. Type the attendee's name to confirm and submit. The refund is issued through your payment provider (Stripe or Square) and is always a full refund.
guide.q.refund_all_attendees: How do I refund all attendees for an listing?
guide.a.refund_all_attendees: On the listing page, click Refund All in the navigation bar. Type the listing name to confirm. Each attendee with a recorded payment is refunded one by one. If some refunds fail (e.g. a payment was already refunded via the provider dashboard), you'll see a summary of how many succeeded and how many failed.
guide.a.partial_refunds: No. The system always issues a full refund for the total amount paid. If you need to issue a partial refund, do it directly through your payment provider's dashboard (Stripe or Square).
guide.q.attendee_after_refund: What happens to the attendee after a refund?
guide.a.attendee_after_refund: The attendee remains registered. A refund only clears their payment record — it does not remove them from the listing. If you also want to remove them, delete the attendee separately after refunding.
guide.q.refund_free_listing: Can I refund an attendee who booked a free listing?
guide.a.refund_free_listing: No. The Refund button only appears for attendees who have a recorded payment. Free-listing attendees have no payment to refund.
guide.a.refund_fails: The most common reason is that the payment was already refunded directly through Stripe or Square. You'll see an error message like "Refund failed. The payment may have already been refunded." If your payment provider is no longer configured, refunds will also fail because there's no provider to process them through.
guide.q.refund_same_attendee_twice: Can I refund the same attendee twice?
guide.a.refund_same_attendee_twice: No. After a successful refund the attendee's payment record is cleared, so the Refund button no longer appears. If you attempt to refund a payment that was already refunded via the provider dashboard, the provider will reject it and you'll see a failure message.
guide.a.how_daily_listings_work: Daily listings let attendees choose a specific date when booking. You set which days of the week are available (e.g. Monday to Friday) and how far in advance people can book. The capacity limit applies independently to each date.
guide.a.what_are_bookable_days: These are the days of the week your daily listing runs on. If you only tick Monday and Wednesday, those are the only days that appear in the date picker. Weekends and unticked days are skipped.
guide.a.what_are_holidays: Holidays are date ranges when no daily listings can be booked. Add them from the Holidays page. Any dates falling within a holiday range are removed from the booking calendar for all daily listings.
guide.a.how_checkin_works: Each ticket has a unique QR code. When an attendee arrives, they show their QR code to a member of staff. The staff member scans it (or opens the link), which takes them to the check-in page. If logged in as an admin, they'll see the attendee's details and ticket quantity, with a button to check them in or out.
guide.a.qr_code_purpose: The QR code links to the ticket's check-in page. Scanning it opens the page in a browser. Non-admin visitors see a message to show the code to staff. Admin visitors see the attendee's details and a check-in button.
guide.q.use_qr_scanner: How do I use the QR scanner?
guide.a.use_qr_scanner: Open an listing and click Scanner. Tap Start Camera to begin (grants camera permission on first use). Point the camera at an attendee's QR code and check-in happens automatically. The scanner works best with the rear camera on mobile devices.
guide.q.scanner_no_checkout: Why doesn’t the scanner check people out?
guide.a.scanner_no_checkout: The scanner is intentionally one-way: it only checks people in, never out. This prevents accidental check-outs from double-scans at a busy door. To check someone out, use the manual check-in page instead.
guide.q.qr_different_listing: What if a QR code is for a different listing?
guide.a.qr_different_listing: If you scan a ticket registered for a different listing, you'll be prompted to confirm before checking them in. This lets you handle last-minute listing changes without turning anyone away.
guide.q.scanner_status_messages: What do the scanner status messages mean?
guide.a.scanner_status_messages: Checked in — shows the attendee's name and ticket count (e.g. "Jo checked in (2 tickets)"). Already checked in — they were already marked as arrived. Refunded — the attendee has been refunded and cannot be checked in. Ticket not found — the QR code doesn't match any registration. Different listing — a confirmation dialogue asks whether to check them in anyway. ID verification — for non-transferable listings, staff are asked to confirm the attendee's ID matches the ticket name before check-in proceeds.
guide.a.what_is_apple_wallet: When configured, attendees see an Add to Apple Wallet button on their ticket page. Tapping it downloads a .pkpass file that adds the ticket to their iPhone Wallet and Apple Watch. The pass shows the listing name, date, location, ticket quantity, price paid, and a QR code for check-in.
guide.q.setup_apple_wallet: How do I set up Apple Wallet?
guide.a.setup_apple_wallet:
Apple Wallet is already configured by your server administrator using pass type {hostAppleWalletPassTypeId}. The Add to Apple Wallet button should appear automatically on all ticket pages. You can override this by entering your own credentials in Settings.

Go to Settings, click Advanced Settings, and find the Apple Wallet section. You need five values from your Apple Developer account:

  1. Pass Type ID — e.g. pass.com.example.tickets
  2. Team ID — your Apple Developer Team ID
  3. Signing Certificate — PEM-encoded certificate for your Pass Type ID
  4. Signing Key — PEM-encoded private key for the certificate
  5. WWDR Certificate — Apple's intermediate certificate (download from the Apple Developer portal)

All five fields are required. Once saved, the Add to Apple Wallet button appears automatically on all ticket pages. If none are configured, the feature is simply hidden.
guide.a.wallet_passes_update: Yes. Apple Wallet periodically polls the server (roughly once a day) and re-downloads the pass with the latest details. There are no push notifications — updates arrive on Apple's polling schedule. Attendees can also pull down on the pass in Wallet to force an immediate refresh.
guide.q.owner_vs_manager: What’s the difference between an owner and a manager?
guide.a.owner_vs_manager: Owners have full access: listings, calendar, groups, users, settings, holidays, sessions, and the activity log. Managers can see listings, the calendar, groups, and the activity log. They cannot change settings, manage users, or view sessions.
guide.q.invite_admin: How do I invite another admin?
guide.a.invite_admin: Go to Users, enter a username and choose their role (owner or manager). You'll receive an invite link to send them. They use the link to set their password and activate their account.
guide.q.invite_link_expiry: How long do invite links last?
guide.a.invite_link_expiry: Invite links expire after 7 days. If the link expires before the person uses it, you'll need to delete the pending user and create a new invite.
guide.a.attendee_data_protection: All personal information (names, email addresses, phone numbers, postal addresses) is encrypted before being stored. Even if the database were compromised, the data cannot be read without the encryption keys. Data is only decrypted when an authenticated admin views it.
guide.q.lost_password: What happens if I lose my password?
guide.a.lost_password: There is no password recovery. If you lose your password, you cannot log in or decrypt any data. Keep your password safe. Another owner can delete your account and send a fresh invite, and all existing attendee data remains accessible to other admins.
guide.a.export_attendee_data: Yes. On any listing's attendee list, click Export CSV . The export includes name, email, phone, address, special instructions, quantity, registration date, amount paid, transaction ID, check-in status, and ticket URL. For daily listings, you can filter by date before exporting.
guide.a.reset_database: It permanently deletes everything: all listings, attendees, users, payment configuration, and sessions. The system returns to its initial setup state. You must type a confirmation phrase to proceed. This cannot be undone.
guide.a.what_are_webhooks: Webhooks send an automatic notification to a URL of your choice whenever someone registers for an listing. You can use them to connect to other services, e.g. sending a Slack message or updating a spreadsheet.
guide.q.setup_webhook: How do I set up a webhook?
guide.a.setup_webhook: Add a webhook URL when creating or editing an listing. Every time someone books that listing, a POST request is sent to your URL with the attendee's details.
guide.q.webhook_json_format: What does the webhook JSON look like?
guide.a.webhook_json_format:
Each webhook is an HTTP POST with Content-Type: application/json. Here is an example payload for a paid listing booking: 

 {webhookExampleJson}

Prices are in the smallest currency unit (e.g. pence for GBP, cents for USD). The ticket_url links to the attendee's ticket page. For multi-listing bookings the tickets array contains one entry per listing and the URL combines all tokens with +.
guide.a.what_are_sessions: A session is created each time an admin logs in. Sessions expire after 24 hours. You can view all active sessions from the Sessions page.
guide.q.logout_other_users: How do I log out other users?
guide.a.logout_other_users: On the Sessions page, click "Log out of all other sessions". This ends every session except your own, forcing all other admins to log in again. Useful if you suspect an account has been compromised.
guide.a.what_is_calendar: The Calendar page lets you pick a date and see every attendee booked across all listings on that day. This is especially useful for daily listings. You can export a CSV of the day's attendees and manage check-ins, edits, and deletions from the same view.
guide.a.what_is_activity_log: The Log page shows a chronological list of admin actions such as listing creation, attendee changes, and settings updates. Both owners and managers can view the log. Each listing also has its own log, accessible from the listing page, showing only actions related to that listing.
guide.a.what_are_email_notifications: When configured, the system can send up to two emails after each successful registration: a confirmation email to the attendee (if they provided an email address) with their ticket details and link, and a notification email to the business email address (if one is set) letting you know someone has booked. Emails are sent in the background and won't delay the booking process.
guide.a.supported_email_providers:
Five providers are supported, all using HTTP APIs (no SMTP required):

  • Resend
  • Postmark
  • SendGrid
  • Mailgun (US)
  • Mailgun (EU)

All providers work the same way — choose whichever you already have an account with or whichever offers a free tier that suits your volume.
guide.q.setup_email: How do I set up email?
guide.a.setup_email:
Email is already configured by your server administrator using {hostEmailProvider} with from address {hostEmailFromAddress}. You can override this by entering your own provider and API key in Settings. If you provide your own settings, they take priority over the server configuration.

  1. Go to Settings and find the Email section
  2. Choose your email provider from the dropdown
  3. Paste your provider's API key into the API Key field
  4. Enter a From Address — this is the sender address that appears on outgoing emails. If left blank, the business email address is used instead
  5. Save the settings

The from address must be a verified sender in your email provider's account, otherwise emails will be rejected. Check your provider's documentation for how to verify a sender domain or address.
guide.q.test_email_working: How do I test that email is working?
guide.a.test_email_working: After saving your email settings, a Send Test Email button appears. Click it to send a test email to your business email address. If the test fails, you'll see an error with the HTTP status code from your provider — check that your API key is correct and your from address is verified.
guide.q.email_not_configured: What if email is not configured?
guide.a.email_not_configured: Email is entirely optional. If no provider is selected, the system skips sending emails silently. Registrations, payments, webhooks, and everything else continue to work normally.
guide.q.confirmation_email_content: What does the confirmation email contain?
guide.a.confirmation_email_content: The attendee receives an email with the listing name(s), quantity, price paid, and a clickable link to their ticket page. For multi-listing bookings, all listings are listed in a single email. The business email is set as the reply-to address so attendees can reply directly to you.
guide.q.admin_notification_email_content: What does the admin notification email contain?
guide.a.admin_notification_email_content: You receive an email showing the attendee's name, email, phone, address, and any special instructions, along with the listing name(s), quantity, and price. The attendee's email is set as the reply-to address so you can reply directly to them.
guide.q.customise_emails: Can I customise the emails that are sent?
guide.a.customise_emails:
Yes. In Settings, scroll to the email template sections. You can customise both the confirmation email (sent to the attendee) and the admin notification email (sent to you). Each has three parts: subject line, HTML body, and plain text body.

Templates use Liquid syntax. Clear any field to revert to the built-in default.
guide.q.template_variables: What variables can I use in templates?
guide.a.template_variables:
• {{ event_names }} — all listing names joined with “and”
  • {{ ticket_url }} — link to view tickets
  • {{ currency }} — currency code (e.g. GBP)
  • {{ attendee.name }}, {{ attendee.email }}, {{ attendee.phone }}, {{ attendee.address }}, {{ attendee.special_instructions }}
  • {{ attendee.quantity }}, {{ attendee.price_paid }}, {{ attendee.date }}

For multi-listing bookings, loop through the entries array: {% for entry in entries %}. Each entry has entry.listing.name, entry.attendee.quantity, etc.
guide.a.template_filters:
Two custom filters are built in:

  • {{ amount | currency }} — formats a number as currency (e.g. £15.00)
  • {{ count | pluralize: "ticket", "tickets" }} — returns the singular or plural form based on the count
guide.q.template_error: What happens if my template has an error?
guide.a.template_error: If a custom template fails to render, the system falls back to the built-in default template automatically. The email is still sent — your attendees won't miss their confirmation.
guide.q.setup_custom_domain: How do I set up a custom domain?
guide.a.setup_custom_domain: If your site runs on Bunny CDN and the BUNNY_API_KEY environment variable is configured, you'll see a Custom Domain section in Settings. Enter your domain (e.g. tickets.yourdomain.com) and save, then follow the CNAME instructions shown and click Validate.
guide.a.what_does_validation_do: Validation checks that your DNS is set up correctly, registers the hostname with Bunny CDN, requests a free SSL certificate, and enables HTTPS. You can re-validate at any time if you change your DNS.
guide.a.available_settings:
The Settings page (owners only) lets you configure:

  • Country — sets your timezone, currency, and phone prefix in one step
  • Business email — included in webhook notifications
  • Payment provider — Stripe, Square, or none
  • Booking fee — percentage-based fee added to ticket prices (requires a payment provider)
  • Embed hosts — restrict which websites can embed your booking forms
  • Terms and conditions — attendees must agree before booking
  • Show public site — enable or disable the public-facing website
  • Site theme — light or dark
  • Admin password — change your login password
guide.a.advanced_settings:
The main Settings page has a link to Advanced Settings for less common configuration. Advanced settings include:

  • Public API — enable the JSON API for external integrations
  • Apple Wallet — configure pass signing certificates for Add to Apple Wallet
  • Email provider — choose and configure your email sending service
  • Email templates — customise confirmation and admin notification emails using Liquid syntax
  • Custom domain — set up a custom domain for your site (Bunny CDN only)
  • Database reset — permanently delete all data
guide.a.what_is_debug_page: The debug page at /admin/debug shows the configuration status of all integrated services (payments, email, Apple Wallet, storage, CDN, notifications) without revealing any secrets or API keys. It's useful for troubleshooting setup issues — you can quickly see which services are configured and which are missing.
guide.a.listing_feeds:
When the public site is enabled, two machine-readable feeds are available:

  • ICS calendar — /feeds/listings.ics — subscribe from any calendar app (Google Calendar, Apple Calendar, Thunderbird, etc.)
  • RSS feed — /feeds/listings.rss — subscribe from any RSS reader

Both feeds include all active listings with open registration. They update automatically as you add, change, or close listings.
guide.q.connect_to_mobilizon: How do I connect to Mobilizon?
guide.a.connect_to_mobilizon:
Mobilizon is a federated listing platform. You can use its built-in importer to pull listings from your ICS feed:

  1. On your Mobilizon instance, go to the listing import tool (or use the public importer at import.mobilizon.fr)
  2. Enter your ICS feed URL: https://{domain}/feeds/listings.ics
  3. Set joinMode to external so the “Join” button on Mobilizon links back to your registration page

Listings will appear on Mobilizon and federate across the Fediverse. Users click through to your site to register and pay.
guide.a.what_is_public_api: The system includes a JSON API that exposes the same data and booking functionality as the web interface. It lets you build custom frontends, integrate with other services, or automate bookings. No API key is needed — the API is public, just like the booking forms.
guide.a.available_endpoints:
The base URL is your domain (e.g. https://{domain}). All responses are JSON.

  • GET /api/listings — list all active, non-hidden listings
  • GET /api/listings/:slug — get a single listing by its slug (hidden listings are accessible if you know the slug)
  • GET /api/listings/:slug/availability?quantity=N&date=YYYY-MM-DD — check if spots are available
  • POST /api/listings/:slug/book — create a booking

All endpoints support CORS, so you can call them from any website. OPTIONS preflight requests are handled automatically.
guide.a.list_listings_api:
GET /api/listings

Response:
${apiListExampleJson}

Prices are in the smallest currency unit (e.g. pence for GBP, cents for USD). maxPurchasable is 0 when the listing is sold out or registration is closed.
guide.q.get_single_listing_api: How do I get a single listing?
guide.a.get_single_listing_api:
GET /api/listings/summer-workshop

Response:
${apiSingleExampleJson}

The availableDates field is only included for daily listings. Returns { "error": "Listing not found" } with status 404 if the listing doesn't exist or is inactive.
guide.a.check_availability_api:
GET /api/listings/summer-workshop/availability?quantity=2

Response:
${apiAvailabilityExampleJson}

For daily listings, add &date=YYYY-MM-DD to check a specific date. The quantity parameter defaults to 1.
guide.q.create_booking_api: How do I create a booking?
guide.a.create_booking_api:
POST /api/listings/summer-workshop/book
Content-Type: application/json

${apiBookRequestJson}

Which fields are required depends on the listing's field settings. The name field is always required. date is required for daily listings (use a date from availableDates). customPrice is for pay-more listings only (in major currency units, e.g. 10.00 for £10).

Free listing response: 

 {apiBookFreeExampleJson}

Paid listing response: 

 {apiBookPaidExampleJson}

Redirect the user to checkoutUrl to complete payment. Possible error responses: 400 (validation error or registration closed), 404 (listing not found), 409 (not enough spots available).
guide.q.api_data_exposure: What data does the API expose?
guide.a.api_data_exposure: The API exposes exactly the same data as the public booking pages — no more. Internal fields like capacity limits, attendee counts, close times, and webhook URLs are never included. The isSoldOut, isClosed, and maxPurchasable fields are derived values, not raw database fields.
guide.a.customise_system: Absolutely. This is open-source software, so you have full control. You can grab the code from GitHub and host it yourself on Bunny Edge Scripting or any compatible platform. The README has deployment instructions to get you started.
guide.q.customise_for_me: Can you customise it for me?
guide.a.customise_for_me: Yes. I offer customisation at a transparent flat rate — see chobble.com/prices for current pricing. I can help you with custom features, branding, listing image design, hosting setup, or whatever you need. You'll own the code outright, and I can show you how to maintain it yourself or handle updates for you. Over 20 years building web systems means I can usually solve problems quickly and clearly.
guide.q.hosting_and_images: Do you help with hosting and images?
guide.a.hosting_and_images: Yes to both. I can set you up on your own Bunny CDN account (or another host) and handle the technical configuration. I also design listing images if you need them. Get in touch and we'll figure out exactly what you need.
guide.demo_mode_notice: Demo Mode — entered text is replaced with sample data
